notes: Codman leveraged his End Result principles to help establish the ACS Hospital Standardization Program, the precursor to the Joint Commission.

target_node: Massachusetts General Hospital End Result System
notes: Codman instituted the End Result System to systematically track patient outcomes, serving as the intellectual genesis for all modern surgical quality improvement.

target_node: The Dartmouth Institute for Health Policy and Clinical Practice
notes: Wennberg established the institute to formalize the study of surgical practice variation, fundamentally shifting outcomes science toward population-level epidemiology.

target_node: VA National Surgical Quality Improvement Program
notes: Khuri led the development and validation of the VA NSQIP, demonstrating that systematic risk-adjusted outcomes measurement could tangibly reduce surgical mortality.

target_node: The Dartmouth Institute for Health Policy and Clinical Practice
notes: Flum's surgical outcomes research methodology was heavily influenced by Wennberg's practice variation framework at Dartmouth, creating the intellectual bridge between epidemiologic outcomes science and clinical surgical quality programs.
Kehlet founded the study group based on his pioneering research into multimodal surgical rehabilitation, challenging traditional perioperative dogma.

source_node: VA National Surgical Quality Improvement Program
target_node: ACS National Surgical Quality Improvement Program
notes: The methodology developed within the VA system was successfully transitioned and scaled into the private sector under the governance of the American College of Surgeons.

target_node: ACS National Surgical Quality Improvement Program
notes: The ACS established the infrastructure to house and govern the private-sector expansion of NSQIP, standardizing clinical data collection globally.

target_node: ACS National Surgical Quality Improvement Program
notes: Ko directed the deployment and refinement of ACS NSQIP, architecting its role as the definitive standard for surgical risk adjustment in civilian hospitals.

source_node: Society of American Gastrointestinal and Endoscopic Surgeons
evidence_locator: SAGES FLS Program (flsprogram.org); SAGES Wiki (sages.org/wiki/fundamentals-laparoscopic-surgery/); PMC5732857
notes: SAGES developed and launched the Fundamentals of Laparoscopic Surgery (FLS) program in 2004 as a comprehensive educational and assessment tool for basic laparoscopic skills. The FLS committee was originally formed by SAGES in 1990 to develop educational materials. The program incorporated the McGill Inanimate System for Training and Evaluation of Laparoscopic Skills (MISTELS) as its manual skills component. ACS joined SAGES in 2005 as a joint educational partner. FLS is designed to be non-procedure-specific and applicable across all surgical specialties using laparoscopy. Since 2004, over 13,000 surgeons have successfully completed the FLS examination. FLS became the first simulation-based assessment tool mandated for board certification in any surgical specialty — a paradigm shift in surgical education from time-based to competency-based training.
Flum drove the formation of the SOC to establish a dedicated academic society supporting the methodology and dissemination of surgical health services research.

target_node: ACS National Surgical Quality Improvement Program
notes: Flum integrated his outcomes science expertise into the ongoing development of ACS NSQIP and SCOAP, solidifying the bridge between academic societies and data registries.

source_node: ACS National Surgical Quality Improvement Program
target_node: Johns Hopkins Hospital Department of Surgery
notes: Johns Hopkins integrated ACS NSQIP into its core departmental governance, aligning Halstedian training structures with modern empirical outcomes tracking.

source_node: ACS National Surgical Quality Improvement Program
evidence_locator: Mayo Clinic Quality and Outcomes Archives
notes: The Mayo Clinic adopted NSQIP standards to govern programmatic quality improvement across its multi-site surgical network.
The ACS participated in the multi-stakeholder governance of SCIP alongside CMS and the Joint Commission, connecting federal quality mandates to the surgical society governance structure.

evidence_locator: ACS/SAGES Joint Statement on FLS Completion (facs.org/about-acs/statements/joint-statement-by-the-acs-and-sages-on-fls-completion); PMC5732857
notes: Beginning with the 2009-2010 academic year, the ABS required all general surgery residents to successfully complete the FLS exam to be eligible to take the ABS Qualifying Exam in Surgery. This made FLS the first validated, simulation-based assessment tool mandated for board certification in any surgical discipline. The ABS also subsequently mandated the Fundamentals of Endoscopic Surgery (FES), another SAGES program, for certification. Edge type 'programmatic_accreditation' captures FLS serving as a mandatory credentialing gateway for ABS certification. This edge creates a modern cross-link between the MIS/endoscopic lineage (SAGES, degree 5) and the certification/governance lineage (ABS, degree 4), bridging two previously unconnected clusters.
Ljungqvist co-founded and formalized the ERAS Society, accelerating the global implementation and auditing of enhanced recovery protocols.
The informal academic study group evolved into a formal global governance body with strict auditing and accreditation protocols.

source_node: Society of American Gastrointestinal and Endoscopic Surgeons
notes: The Fundamentals of Endoscopic Surgery (FES) program was created by SAGES as the flexible endoscopy equivalent of the Fundamentals of Laparoscopic Surgery (FLS) program. FES is a comprehensive educational and assessment tool designed to teach and evaluate the fundamental knowledge, clinical judgment, and technical skills required in the performance of basic gastrointestinal flexible endoscopy. FES uses a virtual reality simulator (GI Bronch Mentor or GI Express) for the manual skills assessment, unlike FLS which uses a physical box trainer. The FES program was developed in collaboration with the ABS, ASCRS, ASMBS, and SSAT. SAGES launched FES publicly in 2014 and it was subsequently mandated by the ABS as part of the ABS Flexible Endoscopy Curriculum. Source: Marks JM et al. Why fundamentals of endoscopic surgery (FES)? Surg Endosc. 2014;28(3):701-703. This edge parallels the SAGES→FLS (2004) edge already in the graph.

evidence_locator: MD Anderson Cancer Center Quality Archives
notes: MD Anderson achieved formal validation for implementing standardized ERAS protocols, particularly bridging oncologic resection with rapid physiological recovery.

Beginning with the 2017-2018 academic year, the American Board of Surgery required all general surgery certification candidates to have completed the ABS Flexible Endoscopy Curriculum, which includes successful completion of the FES program as its Level V milestone. The ABS Flexible Endoscopy Curriculum was developed by the ABS in collaboration with SAGES, ASCRS, ASMBS, and SSAT. This is the second simulation-based assessment mandated by the ABS for board certification, following FLS (mandated 2009, already in graph as FLS→ABS edge). Together, the FLS→ABS (2009) and FES→ABS (2018) edges document the ABS's progressive incorporation of simulation-based competency assessment into board certification — a fundamental shift in how surgical competence is evaluated. Source: ABS Flexible Endoscopy Curriculum official page and SAGES press release, March 2014.

evidence_locator: ACS Geriatric Surgery Verification Program (facs.org/quality-programs/accreditation-and-verification/geriatric-surgery-verification/); Optimal Resources for Geriatric Surgery 2019 Standards
notes: The ACS Geriatric Surgery Verification (GSV) Program launched in July 2019 with 30 evidence-based standards for optimizing surgical care of older adults. Developed by the ACS Geriatric Surgery Task Force in collaboration with over 50 stakeholder organizations and supported by the John A. Hartford Foundation. The ACS GSV Pilot Project (2014-2019) used ACS NSQIP infrastructure to collect geriatric-specific variables across 23 hospitals. In January 2025, CMS mandated reporting on the Age-Friendly Hospital Measure, and GSV created a new 'Age-Friendly Level' to align with this requirement. The GSV represents the newest evolution of the ACS quality verification model: COT Verification (trauma, 1987) → NSQIP (outcomes, 2004) → TQIP (trauma quality, 2008) → MBSAQIP (bariatric, 2012) → GSV (geriatric, 2019). This edge is the most recent in the entire graph (2019) and brings the temporal range into the 2020s via the CMS alignment.
